_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to analyse survey reports from digital media to evaluate how data was collected and whether conclusions are valid.

_source.w: We are learning to analyse survey reports from digital media to evaluate how data was collected and whether conclusions are valid.
y: In an era of abundant data, the ability to critically evaluate statistical claims in media is one of the most important skills for informed citizenship.
Understanding how data collection methods affect the validity of conclusions protects us from being manipulated by misleading statistics.
These analytical skills are essential for evaluating health claims, political polling, advertising and social research.
eg: Evaluating an online survey report: 'Was the sample random? Was the sample size adequate? Could there be selection bias?'
Identifying how a misleading graph in a news article exaggerates a trend by using a truncated axis.
Assessing whether a correlation reported in media implies causation and identifying possible confounding variables.
Evaluating: 'A health article claims a supplement reduces cold duration by 50%. What was the study design?'
Comparing two media reports about the same study and identifying how different framing leads to different conclusions.
Discussing how social media algorithms can create the appearance of consensus through selective data presentation.
